title: Gift Wrapping Station Wall Shelf Organizer
summary: A wall-mounted shelf with drilled holes and dowels that hold wrapping paper rolls upright and organized, plus shelves for tape, scissors, and ribbon.
description:
The dowel holes are the one detail that needs real precision — bored with a spade or Forstner bit sized exactly to a 3/4" dowel, since a loose hole lets the dowel (and every roll of wrapping paper hanging on it) tip and slide. Mark the hole centers carefully before drilling, and cut the corners off the top and bottom afterward for a softer profile.

Everything else is straightforward shelf-and-divider construction, screwed to studs through the back panel since a fully loaded wrapping station — paper rolls, ribbon, tape, scissors — carries real weight for a wall-mounted piece.

tools.note: A 3/4" spade or Forstner bit is what actually matters here — size it exactly to your dowels.
Softens the top and bottom corners after drilling.

cutList.note: Mark and drill the 3/4" dowel holes before cutting the top and bottom corners.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Every wooden part is 1x6, so only the lengths change: two sides at 34", four main shelf-and-back pieces at 31", one divider at 11-1/4", and one small shelf at 19-1/4". Those eight pieces pack into exactly the three supplied 8 ft boards allowing 1/8" per saw cut — cut the two 34" sides first, then the four 31" pieces, and take the two short parts from what is left. Cut four dowels 33" long from the 36" stock. Leave the sides' corners square for now: the dowel holes get drilled before the corners come off, because a square board is far easier to clamp and register than one with its corners already cut away.

steps.title: Drill the dowel holes
steps.body: Clamp the two 34" side panels together with their ends flush and mark every hole centre across both boards at once — a dowel needs its two holes at exactly the same height or every roll on it hangs crooked. The plan does not give the hole spacing, so lay out four evenly spaced centres along the 34" length, keeping each at least a full dowel diameter in from the board edges so the hole does not break out. Drill with a 3/4" spade or Forstner bit sized exactly to your dowels; a loose hole lets the dowel and everything hanging on it tip. Drill all the way through and back the work with scrap so the bit does not blow out the far face — the dowels are 33", about 1/2" longer than the case measures across the outside, so each one passes through both sides and stands roughly 1/4" proud. Cut the top and bottom corners off each side with a jigsaw afterwards for a softer profile.

steps.title: Build the case
steps.body: Join the two 34" sides to the top and bottom — two of the four 31" pieces — with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, keeping the outside faces flush. Work on a flat surface, measure both diagonals corner to corner, and adjust until they match before the glue grabs; the holes you have already drilled will only line up for a straight dowel if this case is square. Clamp or weight it while it sets.

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: The cut list groups four 31" pieces together as 'main shelves and back' without saying how many are which, so decide now: with two used as the top and bottom, you have two 31" boards left to serve as an inner shelf and as the back rail the unit hangs from. Mark the shelf height on both sides at once so it lands level, then glue and nail through the sides into the shelf's ends with 1-1/4" brads. Leave enough clear height below the shelf for the tallest roll of paper you mean to hang. Note that a single 5-1/2"-wide 1x6 is a rail, not a full back panel — this unit has an open back, and its holding power comes from the screws into the studs, not from a sheet.

steps.title: Add the divider
steps.body: The 11-1/4" divider stands vertically between two shelves and splits that compartment in two. Set it where you want the split, mark its position on the shelf above and the shelf below so it goes in plumb, and hold it against a square while you glue and nail through both shelves into its ends with 1-1/4" brads. A divider that leans will not take the small shelf squarely in the next step.

steps.title: Add the remaining shelf
steps.body: The 19-1/4" small shelf spans from one side panel across to the divider, filling the wider of the two bays the divider created. Mark it level on both the side and the divider, then glue and nail through each into the shelf ends with 1-1/4" brads. Because it is nailed into the divider's face rather than through it, drive those brads at a slight angle so they bite rather than slipping along the grain.

steps.title: Hang and add dowels
steps.body: Find the studs first and drive the 3" self-tapping screws through the back rail into solid framing — a loaded wrapping station carries real weight for a wall-mounted piece and drywall anchors will not hold it. Drive one screw, level the unit, then drive the rest. Slide the 33" dowels through the drilled holes last; they carry the paper rolls and are meant to pull straight back out when a roll runs out, so leave them dry rather than gluing them in.

images.alt: Wall-mounted gift wrapping station with dowel rod holders
